name: Greece
summary: Greece is a warm Mediterranean country with significant altitude and maritime influence, producing high-acid wines from indigenous grape varieties.
examTips: Despite warm climate, Greece often produces high-acid wines.
Altitude and wind are key balancing factors.
Assyrtiko, Xinomavro and Agiorgitiko are the key grapes.

santorini.name: Santorini
santorini.summary: Volcanic island producing high-acid Assyrtiko wines despite hot conditions.
santorini.styleSummary: Dry, high-acid white wines with mineral character.
examTips: High acidity in a hot climate is the key clue.
Volcanic soils and wind are major factors.

Assyrtiko.summary: Signature Greek white grape producing dry, high-acid wines with citrus and mineral notes.
Assyrtiko.style: Citrus, saline, mineral, very high acidity.
examTips: High acidity despite heat is key exam clue.

santorini.classification: Santorini is a PDO strongly associated with Assyrtiko and volcanic island viticulture.
lawNotes: Basket-trained vines protect grapes from strong winds.
High acidity despite hot conditions is the key tasting and theory clue.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Wind can cool vineyards and reduce fungal disease pressure, but can also affect vigor and fruit set.
Soil type influences drainage, heat retention, and vine vigor, shaping the style and consistency of the wines.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Training systems, row orientation, and site protection matter where wind is a major factor.
Training system and vine age are important human choices that shape yield, resilience, and fruit concentration.

naoussa.name: Naoussa
naoussa.summary: Northern Greek region producing structured, high-acid red wines from Xinomavro.
naoussa.styleSummary: High-acid, structured reds with ageing potential.

Xinomavro.summary: High-acid, high-tannin grape producing structured, ageworthy red wines.
Xinomavro.style: Red fruit, tomato, olive, high tannin and acidity.

naoussa.classification: Naoussa PDO is one of the key quality homes of Xinomavro.
lawNotes: Xinomavro is often compared with Nebbiolo because of high acidity and tannin.
Structured ageworthy reds are the classic style here.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

nemea.name: Nemea
nemea.summary: Warm Peloponnese region producing soft, fruity red wines.
nemea.styleSummary: Ripe, fruity reds with softer tannin.

Agiorgitiko.summary: Soft, fruity red grape producing medium-bodied wines.

nemea.classification: Nemea PDO is the classic quality appellation for Agiorgitiko.
lawNotes: Agiorgitiko generally gives softer fruitier wines than Xinomavro.
Altitude differences within the region can affect style and freshness.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

amyndeon.name: Amyndeon
amyndeon.summary: Cooler high-altitude region producing fresher styles of Xinomavro.

Xinomavro.style: Red fruit, lighter body, higher acidity.

amyndeon.classification: Amyndeon PDO is a cooler higher-altitude Xinomavro region, known for fresher lighter expressions and also sparkling production context.
lawNotes: Altitude and cooler conditions are the key style drivers.
Compared with Naoussa, Xinomavro is usually lighter and fresher here.
vineyardFactors: Cooler conditions slow ripening, preserve acidity, and increase the importance of site selection and frost avoidance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Producers often focus on canopy and harvest timing to achieve ripeness without losing freshness in cooler years.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.
